posture.rationale: Buyer posture remains ACCUMULATE because the cross-driver average is 37.0, but energy still leads at 71.7 while crude is down 8.37% over five sessions. That keeps staged coverage ahead of passive waiting without forcing panic fills.

marketPosture.title: Market Posture
marketPosture.body: ACCUMULATE remains active. Average network pressure is 37.0, but energy stress still leads at 71.7 while crude has already dropped 8.37% over five sessions. That argues for staged coverage, not a heroic all-at-once fill.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: State the procurement stance decisively so execution remains disciplined under volatility.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: A clear stance reduces hesitation penalties when market conditions are noisy and timing-sensitive.
quantResearchProtocol: Validate posture strictly as ACCUMULATE, WAIT, or DEFER.
Anchor commentary to buyer timing pressure and coverage risk.
Keep output to one to three sharp sentences.
Use controlled dry humor only when it improves calm and clarity.
inferenceConstraints: Do not use trader-centric language or directional trade jargon.
Do not write paragraph walls or broad macro essays.
Do not overstate certainty when evidence is weakening.
outputRequirements: Explain the operational reason in procurement language.
Include at most one short dry line that stays professional when stress context warrants it.

sourceRecords.recordHint: latest buyer posture enum and rationale
network pressure context supporting stance choice

notes: This card is the operating stance anchor for the full strategy surface.
Stance language remains procurement-first and execution-focused.

contractImpactCalculator.title: Contract Impact Calculator
contractImpactCalculator.body: Staged coverage still wins. Energy stress 71.7 is 34.7 points above the 37.0 network average, but tariff pressure has cooled to 15.8 and crush stays benign at 14.6. One-shot execution would still be paying for rebound risk that has not fully transferred into soybean-oil replacement cost.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Quantify whether current conditions favor staged coverage, single-window execution, or defensive patience for soybean-oil procurement.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When pressure remains concentrated in key channels, staged execution reduces timing asymmetry and buyer regret more effectively than forced one-shot coverage.
quantResearchProtocol: Use average pressure, lead-driver score, and lead-driver spread as the core structure inputs.
Separate staged execution from one-shot execution by comparing latency risk with over-coverage risk.
Keep the recommendation buyer-side and replacement-cost focused.
Define explicit invalidation triggers tied to energy and volatility moderation.
Keep output to one to three executive-readable sentences.
inferenceConstraints: Do not recommend one-shot execution while energy remains the lead channel.
Do not describe staging without quantifying why staging is superior.
Do not present procurement guidance as a trade idea.
Do not drift into hype, meme, or newsletter tone.
outputRequirements: Quote the score math behind the recommendation.
List the conditions that would justify changing the structure.
Include at most one short dry line that reduces tension without diluting clarity.

sourceRecords.recordHint: current risk-factor synthesis used for contract timing
soybean-oil futures daily close used for current buyer cost framing

notes: Contract timing is tied to cross-driver spread math, not generic caution language.
Buyer recommendation stays replacement-cost focused rather than speculative.

factorWaterfall.title: Factor Waterfall
factorWaterfall.body: Pressure stack is energy pass-through (71.7), volatility transmission (43.2), China flow risk (39.5), tariff threat (15.8), then crush pressure (14.6). The stack is concentrated, not broad, so Chris should monitor crude and OVX first and treat the other channels as confirmation rather than the main trigger.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Rank active drivers by marginal buyer impact so monitoring effort stays on the channels that can move replacement cost fastest.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Procurement risk is shaped by channel ordering and spread, not just by the average level of stress.
quantResearchProtocol: Order each driver by current score and note the spread from the network average.
Separate dominant drivers from background channels.
Call out whether the stack is broad or concentrated.
Translate the stack into concrete monitoring priority.
Keep commentary concise and supportive of the chart/card visuals.
inferenceConstraints: Do not collapse the waterfall into an undifferentiated summary.
Do not hide conflict or concentration inside the stack.
Do not assign confidence without freshness context.
Do not restate obvious movement that the graphic already shows.
outputRequirements: State whether the stack is broad or concentrated.
Explain the monitoring consequence for a buyer.
Include at most one cynical but professional line acknowledging market absurdity.

sourceRecords.recordHint: driver ordering and contribution math for current strategy stack

notes: Waterfall ordering is derived from the active driver scores in the refreshed dashboard snapshot.
Dominance language is reserved for channels materially above the network average.

riskMetrics.title: Risk Metrics
riskMetrics.body: Buyer timing asymmetry is moderate but still energy-led. VIX is 16.76, OVX is 73.54, and managed money stays net long 158,107 contracts, or 22.08% of open interest, so a crude rebound can still move soybean-oil replacement cost faster than the average stack score suggests.

strategicSpecialInstructions.cardTopic: Buyer Risk Math and Tail Exposure
strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Express near-term soybean-oil procurement risk in explicit asymmetry and latency-risk terms.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: For buyers, moderate average pressure can still justify action if one live channel remains strong enough to reprice costs faster than the physical market is moving.
quantResearchProtocol: Compare soybean-oil futures movement with crude movement and the network average.
Measure the premium of the lead channel over the stack average.
Classify whether timing risk or over-hedge risk is larger.
Tie the answer to cadence and monitoring rules.
Keep output concise and mathematically grounded.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call risk neutral when one channel is still materially elevated.
Do not omit latency implications from the math.
Do not imply guaranteed cost outcomes.
outputRequirements: State the risk regime in buyer language.
Explain latency risk versus over-coverage risk.
Include at most one short dry line that keeps urgency controlled.

sourceRecords.recordHint: average network risk and lead-driver spread

notes: Risk framing combines active network pressure with weekly positioning rather than treating either one as sufficient.
Latency risk is expressed in buyer cadence terms, not trader return terms.
